data.title: Intuit Has Fallen Too Far (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
The last article that I wrote about Intuit (INTU) was published way back in November of 2024. At that time, I viewed the company in a more neutral light. Even though the business had exhibited strong growth on the top and bottom lines, the stock looked incredibly expensive. At the end of the day, I am a value investor. So I do focus a great deal on the price that I am paying for the perceived value of an opportunity. Looking back, I think I gave the company too much credit for its growth and strong profitability. Instead of rating it a ‘hold,’ I should have called it a ‘sell.’ But the past is the past. Looking at the picture today, things look a lot different. The firm continues to expand rapidly, and the opportunities for it moving forward are appealing. And yet, the stock is down 44.8% since the time that article was published. The market, meanwhile, is up 18.1% over the same window of time. This plunge in share price opens a door of opportunity. And I think that, given how attractive the company is and its operational trajectory, upgrading it to a soft ‘buy’ makes sense here.

Mr. Market has this one wrong

Very few companies are as iconic, in my view, as Intuit. In addition to owning significant platforms like QuickBooks, Credit Karma, and TurboTax, the firm also owns lesser known but still incredibly relevant properties like MailChimp. While these assets might not seem all that appealing to many investors, as somebody who has a history in accountancy and who lives and breathes finance, I find the business riveting. And what's really awesome is that, as of late, the company has exhibited strong upside.

Perhaps the most relevant data to look at would cover through the first half of the 2026 fiscal year. During that time, revenue for the company was $8.54 billion. That represents an increase of 17.8% compared to the $7.25 billion reported a year earlier. As revenue for the company expands, profitability rises also. Net income nearly doubled from $668 million to $1.14 billion. Even if we use adjusted figures provided by management, the rise was from $1.65 billion to $2.10 billion. Operating cash flow for the company expanded from $1.43 billion to $2.21 billion. And if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $1.99 billion to $2.83 billion. Finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from $1.20 billion to $1.72 billion.

The chart above shows financial performance for 2025 as a whole compared to 2024. Revenue, profits, and cash flows, have all been on the rise. This is proof that the 2026 fiscal year is not exceptional in and of itself. Rather, it's part of a continuing trend of expansion for the business. It is this exact trajectory of growth that the firm has been on for a long time now that justified my more neutral stance previously at a time when the stock looked incredibly pricey. However, to really understand what is going on here, it would be best to dig deeper.

The largest part of the company is its Global Business Solutions segment. This consists of QuickBooks and Intuit Enterprise Suite, the combination of which allows the company to serve small and mid market firms across the globe, not to mention accounting professionals. It also engages in other activities here, such as bill pay, merchant payment processing activities, and even the facilitation of financing for small and mid market enterprises.

During the first half of 2026, revenue for this part of the company came in at $6.16 billion. That represents a significant increase over the $5.22 billion generated a year earlier. According to the data provided by management, this rise in revenue was driven by strength across every major sales category for it. Leading the way was its QuickBooks Online Accounting product line, with revenue surging 24.4% from $1.97 billion to $2.45 billion. Management has been able to extract higher prices from existing customers, while simultaneously bringing in new customers. A mix in the shift of offerings also seems to have added to the top line. Meanwhile, its Online Services business line reported a 17.7% increase in revenue from $2.01 billion to $2.36 billion. That rise in revenue can be attributed to increased activity involving its money offerings and payroll offerings. Most of that can be chalked up to greater payment volume per customer, higher prices charged to customers, and other miscellaneous factors.

Other parts of the segment did not perform as well. But even these laggards posted impressive results, all things considered. As an example, QuickBooks Desktop Accounting reported an 11.7% rise in sales from $684 million to $764 million. And its Desktop Services and Supplies business reported a 4.2% increase in revenue from $550 million to $573 million. These increases are the result of higher prices, according to management. As revenue for this segment increased, profitability improved also. Operating income shot up 16.8%, from $4.05 billion to $4.74 billion.

We also have the Consumer segment of the business. This includes TurboTax, Credit Karma, and ProTax. Revenue for this segment grew 17.2%, from $2.03 billion to $2.38 billion. The greatest expansion here was attributable to Credit Karma, with revenue skyrocketing an astounding 25.1%, from $1.01 billion to $1.27 billion. According to the company, the unit enjoyed a surge in sales because of increased activity when it came to not only its personal loan vertical, but also its credit card and insurance verticals. Management has been very active about growing this piece of the business. For instance, the company continues to tie in its different properties.

In the nine months ending in July of last year, for instance, the firm saw a 30% increase in the number of Credit Karma members that filed with TurboTax using the Credit Karma platform. And that has even served to push up revenue for TurboTax itself. In December of last year, Credit Karma and TurboTax launched ‘Now This Is Taxes,’ which was the second year of its brand campaign by that name that the firm is relying on in order to draw in customers primarily aged 18 to 44. This included partnerships with the NCAA, Fortnite Creative, Amazon Prime Video, and other parties, all centered around making taxes easier than they ever have been before. And in February of this year, the firm announced that it partnered with Anthropic to integrate custom AI agents across its network, which includes not only Credit Karma but also other aspects of its ecosystem like TurboTax, QuickBooks, and MailChimp.

AI seems to be a big growth opportunity for the company as opposed to a threat. In its latest earnings call, the firm mentioned that its virtual AI agent team has more than 3 million customers. And repeat engagement is in excess of 85%. The company is also really scaling its Intuit Intelligence, which it describes as a system of intelligence that changes how customers engage with its platform in order to run their businesses. When it comes to Intuit Enterprise Suite, the company is also launching industry specific AI native ERP solutions focused on mid market companies. One thing I really appreciate about the company is that it is not going only in AI. It has specifically mentioned continuing to use human intelligence because of the regulatory intensity of the space in which it operates and because that is what customers expect. This actually matches my own philosophical view of AI. On its own, AI has certain benefits. But it's only when you combine it with the human element that you get to optimize the potential that exists.

Thanks to continued growth that is being fueled at least in large part by innovations, the firm expects continued expansion this year. Management believes that revenue for 2026 will be between $20.997 billion and $21.186 billion. That compares to the $18.83 billion the business generated last year. Adjusted earnings per share should come in at between $22.98 and $23.18. At the midpoint, this would mean adjusted net profits of $6.46 billion. And if we assume that other profitability metrics will rise similarly, we would be looking at adjusted operating cash flow of $7.27 billion and EBITDA of $6.34 billion.

In the chart above, you can see what this would mean for the valuation of the company. The fact of the matter is that shares are priced at levels that would typically fall under the fair value category as I describe it. However, for such a high-quality growth machine that generates significant cash flow, I am certainly happy to be more flexible. In addition to being attractive on an absolute basis, the company is also relatively attractive compared to other similar firms. In that chart, I stacked it up against five similar enterprises. Only one of them was cheaper than it is on a price-to-earnings basis. And only two of them ended up being cheaper when it came to the price-to-operating cash flow and EV-to-EBITDA approaches.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available right now, I believe that the market has pushed shares of Intuit lower than it should have. This plunge in price, however, does offer a good opportunity for investors. The business is by no means a home run in my view. But it is attractive enough to justify an upgrade to a soft ‘buy’ at this time.
